data.title: Civeo Corp.: Increased Demand And Focused Strategy Offer Growth Prospects
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Luke Zwynenburg as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Thesis

Despite widespread uncertainty in Canada and recent disappointing Q4 earnings, which saw the stock drop 13% in a day, Civeo Corp. (NYSE:CVEO) is a unique company that, I feel, is positioned to grow in the coming months and years. I believe Civeo Corp. is set to benefit from increased demand for oil and mining and also presents a targeted strategy to deal with ongoing issues, especially when it comes to Canada.

Overview

Civeo Corp.’s business model focuses on offering housing and accommodations to workers in the energy and construction industries, primarily in Australia and Canada, with some business in the United States. They focus on hard-to-reach remote areas where there may be a lack of resources for workers in the aforementioned sectors. The stock has gone up over 300% in the past 5 years but has disappointed more recently; markedly, it saw a 19% decline over the past year. In their most recent earnings call, they reported misses on revenue, bottom line, and EPS estimates. This was primarily attributed to issues in Canada and a feeling of general economic uncertainty in the region. However, since earnings, the stock was up 14% prior to tariff news, and I feel there are plenty of reasons to be optimistic going forward despite economic uncertainty.

Demand On The Rise

CVEO is set to benefit from increased demand for oil and mining. In order to fight global warming, many precious minerals from discrete places of the world are desired. Many expect demand for key minerals like lithium, cobalt, and graphite to at least double by 2030. To obtain resources, there will be increased mining. Two of the places Civeo does business, the United States and Canada, have some of the largest lithium reserves in the world. Australia is also second in terms of largest cobalt reserves. This is what Civeo Corp. focuses on: providing the workers in these harsh environments lacking resources with the hospitality they need to do their jobs.

With two rate cuts expected this year, history shows that there should be more demand for oil, at least in the short term. Despite increased demand for mining and oil, there appears to be stagnation in the construction market, especially relating to homebuilding. There is generally a lack of consumer confidence in the market, and many homebuilders themselves do not have a positive outlook on the market. However, the success of CVEO isn't contingent solely on the number of housing starts. Their business model is multifaceted, relating to demand for oil, mining, and construction. The data suggest that two of the three of those should experience greater demand in the near future.

Earnings and Guidance Review

In June 2024, CVEO looked in pole position to continue progress after a strong Q2 earnings report where they beat both revenue and EPS estimates. In September, for Q3, they beat again on revenue but largely missed on projected EPS. In February, things were a lot worse; they missed on both revenue and EPS estimates for Q4.

As mentioned previously, this was primarily due to a decline in Canadian revenues. Based on their 10k from 2024 and 2023, they saw a 19.5% decrease in Accommodation and Other Service revenue in Canada, while Australia saw a 10.6% increase in revenue for the same category. Additionally, Total Billed Rooms for Lodges and Villages decreased by 18.6% YoY for Canada, while the same revenue stream increased by 6.4% in Australia. So, what is the company doing to deal with these revenue issues in Canada?

During their most recent earnings call in February, the CEO, Bradley Dawson, said that 2025 in Canada will be a "transition year" for their business. He acknowledged that they will need to restructure their Canadian operations in order to position themselves for future growth. Civeo Corp. projects to do between 630 and 660 million in revenue in 2025; this is compared to 682 million in 2025. This could be discouraging for investors, however, much of what Civeo does is contingent on demand for resources and trade. These factors may not fall into place immediately, hence the uncertainty this year. During the call, the company cited reduced capital spending from customers alongside political and economic uncertainty as the reasons for the decline in Canada. Specifically, they aimed to reduce their dependency on oil sands in Canada and reduce overhead costs in the region by 25%.

The company expects the conditions causing the struggles to persist in Canada this year. On the more positive side, the company iterated that they felt LNG (liquified natural gas) activity would increase in the next couple of years, which will offer another growth driver. The aforementioned "restructuring" and transitions that were discussed do not all necessarily happen this year. That means revenues may not start to rise immediately. However, there is a reason to be optimistic, as Civeo seems to be taking the right steps to address key issues and position itself for future growth.

Additionally, CVEO recently announced plans to buy 10% of common shares. This is generally considered a good thing and could potentially indicate that the company has more free cash flow. The most recent peak on the chart below is the year-end of 2020, and after this, the stock went from around $14 to $31 in two years. Their biggest stock buyback before 2020 was in March 2020 and seems to be correlated with increases in free cash flow.

Competition

Given that CVEO is a unique company, it is hard to identify competitors in the traditional sense. However, there are still certain companies in the hospitality business that can offer a good reflection of where Civeo Corp stands in the broader market. I have conducted a comparison between CVEO and some of its main competitors.

The main thing that stands out in this analysis is the EV/sales ratio. Most of these companies sit within the 1-3 range, which is a good place to be. Anything under one is generally considered undervalued, and this is great news for CVEO. Target hospitality is close in business model to CVEO and is up 18% in the last month at the time of writing.

Potential Risks

Of course, like all companies, Civeo Corp. has risks. I have opted to break this down into both idiosyncratic risk vs systematic risk.

In terms of company risk, there is the large issue of Canada. The company has laid out a clear vision for how to deal with its ongoing issues and continues into a “transition period”. However, if this strategy does not work out, things could get worse in Canada, only hurting future revenues and net income. There is also the possibility that their estimates for LNG activity are off, and they are not able to reduce their oil sands dependency. There remain questions as to how restructuring in Canada will be carried out and if it will bring any additional costs.

In terms of broader market risk, there is the massive question mark of tariffs. Canada and Australia both received tariffs from the US recently. The news further exacerbates economic uncertainty in Canada and adds some tension to a previously rolling Australian market. Some even predict that Australia's GDP could decline by as much as 1%. This would likely decrease oil and mining activity and create a decreased demand for Civeo Corp.’s hospitality services. There is also the possibility that geopolitical events could impact the demand for oil and mining, altering Civeo Corp.’s projections.

Conclusion

While CVEO carries with it certain risks, overall, I feel it offers a strong value proposition due to a unique business model, the ability to adjust strategy to deal with recent declines in Canadian revenues, and relative undervaluation in key metrics compared to competitors.

data.title: Satellogic: U.S. Domestication Is Bullish But Capitalizing On Constellation Is Key (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In December, I covered Satellogic (NASDAQ:SATL) with a strong sell rating. That rating hasn’t been wrong since the stock lost 6% of its value. However, since then, the company has reported its full-year results, which could perhaps provide a more positive indication for the stock.

After writing the report, I also had a conversation with the CFO of Satellogic and the VP of Strategy & Corporate Development. That provided a lot of useful insights that did not become immediately clear from investor presentations. In this report, I will be discussing some items that are worth noting, and I will be updating my price target for Satellogic stock and my rating if appropriate.

For those that are not familiar with Satellogic, it provides geospatial solutions with low acquisition costs as a key selling point. In the future, an additional selling point in the form of daily remap capability could be unlocked.

Satellogic Completes Move To US Domestication

Satellogic has been contemplating a move to become a US registered company for a while, and that move was completed in March 2025. While it may seem like a small change domesticating in the US from the British Virgin Island, the implications could very well be big.

For investors, investing in a company domesticated in the US could be more attractive. So, Satellogic could potentially attract more investors, including investment firms.

Next to that, the US registration gives the company better access to bid for government contracts. Satellogic’s services can offer solutions for defense customers, but also to commercial customers. Especially for the US government contracts, the US domestication was important.

When I spoke to the CFO, he also shared that winning US government contracts could eventually also result in more order activity from other NATO countries.

Satellogic’s Current Constellation Offers Huge Revenue Upside

One positive element from my conversations with Rick Dunn and Ryan Driver from Satellogic was that they highlighted that with the current constellation size they can generate revenues that are ten times the current revenues. That is an important piece of information that the company has not made clear in its investor presentations.

Putting it simple, the revenues are not limited by the constellation size at this point in time and Satellogic's current task is more focused on attracting customers so it can generate more revenues from its current constellation size. Daily remap capability would provide a boost to the addressable market, but it is not what Satellogic really needs to scale revenues.

It seems that loading the network with contract customers is what the company is currently doing. On the 8th of April, a $30 million contract was announced for near-real time global defense monitoring. The company also announced a contract for imagery for the Brazilian Air Force, a contract for oil and natural gas monitoring and a partnership with Maxar that gives Maxar access to Satellogic’s constellation and pairs Maxar’s customer base and demands with Satellogic’s constellation.

Satellogic Operating Loss Improves On Lower Depreciation And Engineering

Satellogic reported revenues of $12.9 million compared to $10.1 million a year ago, driven by higher asset monitoring revenues. While this is a 28% increase in revenues, the significant reduction in the operating loss was not driven by the revenue growth.

The reduction in the operating loss from $69.4 million to $52.2 million was driven by a $7.8 million reduction in engineering costs driven by a workforce rationalization. Furthermore, depreciation expenses decreased by $4.6 million as depreciation expenses in 2023 were higher as 13 satellites reached their end of useful life. SG&A expenses decreased by nearly $2 million. These three items accounted for over 80% of the reduction in operating losses.

Net losses increased from $60.7 million to $116.8 million, driven by fair value adjustments of financial instruments such as warrants. This is a non-cash and non-operational item, so it is not something I am worried about.

Positive Cash Flow Seems To Be Far Away

In 2024, Satellogic seems to have positioned itself for a slower ramp up and that is perfectly fine, especially if we consider that the current constellation size does allow for significantly higher revenues. The company had $23.7 million in cash, but it should be noted that none of its comes from the operating cash flow. The operating cash flow burn decreased from $49.6 million to $35.9 million, primarily reflecting the reduction in the workforce. CapEx decreased from $14.9 million to $5 million.

Previously, my concern with Satellogic was that it almost looked as if the company would require daily remap capability to grow to positive free cash flows. This would require a total constellation size of 200 satellites, costing around $200 million in total CapEx. Satellite decay would also mean that the company would need to keep launching satellites to compensate for decaying satellites. The company currently has around 20 satellites in orbit. This means that around 180 satellites would still need to be launched, with CapEx of around $180 million. I saw no way in which the company would get there given its cash pile of only $20 million to $25 million and negative operating cash flow.

The fact that the company can scale revenues 10x with the current constellation size is a big plus in that regard. However, for the time being the reality remains that the company has a negative free cash burn of almost $41 million dollars which was covered through issuing convertible debt and proceeds from PIPE investments.

The issuance of convertible debt and the negative cash flow does mean that there is a substantial risk that the company may dilute shareholders to fund activities. The company needs to grow its revenues to north of $60 million to break-even on operating loss, which at the current revenue growth rate would only happen after 2030.

So, a significant acceleration in growth is required for the company to significantly reduce dilution risk to shareholders. That simply means that while no major CapEx is required to support revenue growth, the pace of revenue growth is going to be very important to limit shareholder dilution.

What Is Satellogic Stock Worth?

For space companies that are not profitable yet, I am applying a P/S ratio of 12.75x, which is in line with Rocket Lab. I am using that ratio because I do believe that Rocket Lab is the pinnacle of space companies at this moment.

If we apply that to Satellogic at a revenue growth of around 44%, which is in line with the CAGR between 2021 and 2024, we get to a $3.77 price target excluding dilution when valuing the stock one-year ahead of revenues. If Satellogic funded its cash requirements through shareholder dilution, it would bring the price target to $3.19. The current price of $4.47 seems to be more reflective of a price in line with FY27 revenues and provides around 30% downside.

That, however, does not mean that I am maintaining my Strong Sell rating. Over the longer-term, this could be a speculative opportunity. If we look at the 5-year target we would get to $8.82 and if we would only recognize around half of the value by 2029 we get to a $4.41 price target, which is not far off from the current stock price. As a result, I am upgrading the stock from strong sell to hold.

Satellogic Has A Challenging Path Ahead

Satellogic has a nice business, but the reality is that it has a long path ahead to reach break-even levels, and that means that there is dilution risk for shareholders. I do believe that the US domestication provides significant opportunities for Satellogic and the partnership with Maxar could fuel growth.

A lot, however, is going to depend on how fast Satellogic can capitalize on its current constellation. The stock is not a convincing buy, but also not a convincing sell. It is just relatively expensive compared to the FY25 growth that I mapped, and I would classify this more of a speculative buy opportunity for which I generally apply a hold rating. As a result, I am upgrading the stock from strong sell to hold and note the potential risks involved.

data.title: Boston Omaha Holds Many Durable Assets And Businesses
data.description:
Boston Omaha (NYSE:BOC) is a hidden gem that has not been discussed a lot. After my previous article, the businesses keep progressing as planned, showing their resilience under volatile external/macro environments. I think BOC has a group of high-quality and durable holding companies. It has many earnings that will reward investors over time and I would keep my buy rating for this one.

The overall business looks very stable and steadily progresses

BOC's Q4 results look good, and the stock price rose after the earnings release. Their outdoor billboard unit has continued its growth organically without drawing any new capital as its revenue grew 4%, and adjusted EBITA grew 10%. The broadband business continues to grow sales 11% and a large amount of new fiber subscribers (up 63%). Surety bonds business continues to add new premiums written by 40% and grew net income by 32%. Other investments like Sky Harbour and real estate funds at Boston Omaha Asset Management (BOAM) also perform as expected. The net assets of the company continue to grow from 286M in 2020 to 418M in 2024. On a per-share basis, the net assets grew from 11.1 in 2020 to 13.28 in 2024. Overall, I think BOC became a stronger company as a whole after the year of 2024. This is a very stable and well-managed company under CEO Adam Peterson. You rarely see any shocking news or mishaps from the company.

Broadband will be the key source of capital deployment and growth moving forward

According to the CEO, BOC is focusing on internal investments instead of acquiring outside business. This is because their broadband business has a very limited time window as they won’t be able to expand their passing footprints indefinitely. Both companies are laying out fiber passings aggressively, from 26.46K in 2023 to 39.9 K in 2024. These passings are high-capex items but offer extremely long periods of return. I think at least 90% of these passings will be converted to subscribers in the future and could lead to 28M revenue potential for the company.

Because BOC broadbands work in rural or suburban areas, the competition isn’t so concerning once they get installed in a community. Other players have no incentive to come in and compete on price. That will be a lose-lose scenario. The first-mover advantage on this is huge (especially for those HOA deals in master planned communities), although it takes a long time to construct those passings, and the revenue doesn’t come until several years later. You can see this from their Fiber Fast Homes (FFH) business, which adds more passings than subscribers.

Moving forward, I expect the number of fiber passings will continue to grow especially in opportunities that the management found attractive: “through deals with existing manufactured home communities, developer joint ventures, government program awards to build in underserved areas, and/or building within adjacent communities that may have inferior broadband technology options to fiber.”

Economic downturns and tariffs should not be huge issue for BOC

BOC should be very resilient under the current economic uncertainty. The BOC corporate didn’t carry much debt, so not a lot of bankruptcy risk. Many of the assets are backed by real estate markets, and I don’t see real estate value crushing given the asymmetrical supply and demand situation for a long time. Also, many BOC units operate in rural regions, which are mostly manufacturing or agriculture sectors. These are places that the Trump administration wants to protect. The billboard business has been waiting for good billboard deals on lands for several quarters now. A downturn may help them get better deal opportunities and improve margins. Finally, broadband is obviously a critical utility service, which isn’t something people will cut. These BOC-owned businesses are really bulletproof moving forward.

The only concerning part for me is the Surety bonds unit which is growing fast and building scale. However, the fast speed also introduces more underwriting speed as it is very important to keep the loss rate down (currently at 14.5%). As the huge uncertainty introduced by current Trump administration, I think this segment can go towards either positive or negative direction.

Buy BOC is buying CEO Adam Peterson

BOC is a decentralized holding company, specialized in capital deployments. The corporate doesn't really intervene in the day-to-day operations of their holding companies, instead, they make capital deployment decisions on whether or not they should invest in certain businesses or take earnings from them and deploy to areas with higher return. The billboard business is a great example. Several years ago, Link Media was growing very fast, and BOC was super aggressive on acquisitions and invested lots of money there. Now, this business has reached scale and decent profitability. It now takes money from Link and distributes it to their broadband business, where CEO Adam Peterson thinks it can yield a decent long-term return.

The conviction to BOC all comes down to Adam Peterson. He is a hedge fund manager himself, a founder and majority owner. He seems to be a very pragmatic and candid CEO. I especially liked him listing the mistakes he made during the year in his shareholder letters. He also has an excellent track record of finding real estate-related deals to grow capital. Almost all of the current BOC units involve multiple acquisitions, and I don’t see any bad decisions. Cb&T Holdings hasn’t worked well for now, but I think it will turn out to be a successful investment in the end. As Adam continues to deliver returns on his investments, more and more operators are willing to join him based on his reputation, which, in return, offers BOC more wiggle room to diversify and synergize its businesses.

Bottom Line

BOC owns many durable businesses that, I think, will still thrive 10 years from now, and it is not trading in a premium. The cash and net asset value of three operating units (Billboards, insurance, and broadband) totals 418M. Boston Omaha Asset Management (BOAM) investments totaled 133.5M (if we take Sky Harbour market value, that will add 70M+ more). Add all these up, we get 551.5M alone and now the market cap seating at 443m, clearly a discount. From an earnings power standpoint, the three operating businesses are generating 32M EBITA already (excluding FFH). If we deduct BOAM assets from market cap, we get around 310M, which is less than 10X EBITA for the three operating businesses. This doesn’t account for the future conversion of current passings (I estimate it will contribute at least another 10M EBITA). Most importantly, I believe CEO Adam Peterson will do more buybacks in the future, given he already did one in 2024.

data.title: Earnings Insight: Oil Refiners See Sharp Declines To Q1 Estimates
data.description:
By Tajinder Dhillon

Energy companies are facing a double headwind: proposed tariffs that threaten to dampen demand, and an unexpected increase in OPEC production that could raise global supply and place additional downward pressure on oil prices.

Although oil is not directly affected by tariffs, there are second-order effects of potential slower economic growth and reduced consumption. Oil refiners face a lower oil price environment which will compress refining margins.

The three largest refiners in the U.S. — Phillips 66 (PSX), Valero Energy (VLO), and Marathon Petroleum (MPC) - have all seen significant earnings downgrades from sell-side analysts, according to LSEG I/B/E/S.

Using the ‘Screener’ app in LSEG Workspace, estimates for Phillips 66’s upcoming quarter (results due April 25) have been revised down by –166% over the past 30 days — the largest negative revision of any company in the S&P 500. Peers including Marathon have seen similarly sharp revisions (–138%, second largest), while Valero’s estimates have been cut by –49% (Exhibit 1).

As highlighted in our S&P 500 Q1 Earnings Season Preview, the energy sector is expected to report the lowest earnings growth rate in Q1 at -16.9%. Drilling down further, the Oil & Gas Refining & Marketing sub-industry is expected to post the lowest Q1 earnings growth in the entire sector (-101.8%) and a full-year 2025 earnings decline of -17.1%, also the lowest in the group.

Exhibit 1: Largest Negative Revisions to FQ1 Earnings Estimate (30d)

Source: LSEG Workspace

In the image above, we show the StarMine SmartEstimate and the consensus estimate, along with the Predicted Surprise % (PS%), which measures the difference between the StarMine SmartEstimate (a weighted average that emphasizes timely and historically accurate analysts) and the consensus estimate. When PS% is greater than +2% or less than –2%, it correctly forecasts the direction of the earnings surprise approximately 70% of the time.

Throughout the rest of this note, we focus on Phillips 66, which has seen the largest estimate revision to date. However, we would observe a similar pattern from a StarMine perspective when analyzing Marathon Petroleum and Valero. Looking at the Detailed Estimates page in LSEG Workspace, Phillips 66 has a PS% of –108% (Exhibit 2).

The PS% is a valuable tool for investors looking to anticipate earnings surprises and avoid being caught off guard by unexpected misses or beats. The last time Phillips 66 missed earnings was in Q1 2024, which triggered a 7-day post-earnings stock decline of –7.7%. (Exhibit 3).

Exhibit 2: StarMine Predicted Surprise for Phillips 66

Source: LSEG Workspace

Exhibit 3: Historical EPS Surprise – Phillips 66

Source: LSEG Workspace

The Analyst Revision Model (ARM), which gauges analyst sentiment and is predictive of future analyst revisions. Exhibit 4 shows the ARM output for Phillips 66, which captures revisions for three-line items – Revenue, EBITDA, EPS – over three distinct time horizons: current quarter, current year, and next year. The model also incorporates a fourth component: the change in analyst recommendations.

Phillips 66 currently has a current ARM score of 2, placing it in the bottom decile in North America based on regional percentile ranking. The model also offers additional breakdowns by country, sector, and industry to provide further granularity and comparability.

Exhibit 4: StarMine ARM Model – Phillips 66

Source: LSEG Workspace

Buy-side sentiment has also turned sour and is at the lowest level in three years, according to the StarMine Smart Holdings Model, where Phillips 66 has a model score of 6, again in the lowest decile.

The StarMine Smart Holdings (SH) model assesses how a company is aligned to institutional investor preferences based on 25 fixed factors like volume, price momentum, profitability, value, growth, analyst revisions, and leverage.

The SH model is driven by two key components:

  • Institutional Screening: evaluates whether a company aligns with current investor preferences.
  • Change Component: identifies shifts in company fundamentals that could make it more appealing to portfolio managers. A high score suggests that the company is now more likely to appear on the radar of portfolio managers as they run their idea generation screens, making it a potential candidate for increased institutional accumulation.

Breaking down specific factors (Exhibit 5), Phillips 66 has seen deterioration in Leverage ratios, including Interest Coverage, Debt/Assets, and Debt/Equity. Analyst Revisions have seen a fall as already discussed and share prices have fallen, which are captured in the Price Momentum category.

Amongst the more popular factors include EPS Trailing 5-year CAGR with a rank of 6, and has a score of 20 in this factor (higher scores are better). Within the Profitability category, Net Margin is sought after with a rank of 4 and Phillips 66 has a net profit margin of 1.5%.

Exhibit 5: StarMine Smart Holdings Model – Phillips 66

Source: LSEG Workspace

If we take a closer look at the leverage ratios highlighted above, we find that Phillips 66 may be at risk of potential future downgrades to its credit rating. The StarMine Smart Ratios Credit Risk Model incorporates data from the balance sheet, income statement, and key performance indicators (KPIs) to assess the likelihood of future bankruptcy or default risk over a 12-month horizon. The model evaluates a range of metrics including profitability, leverage, coverage, liquidity, and growth & stability, using both trailing 12-month and forward 12-month ratios.

Phillips 66 currently holds a StarMine Implied Rating of BB-, which can be compared to traditional ratings from agencies such as S&P and Moody’s (see Exhibit 6). Empirical research shows that when the StarMine Implied Rating deviates significantly from agency ratings, there is a higher probability that agencies will revise their rating toward the implied level.

The BB- implied rating translates to a numerical probability of default (PD%) of 1.16% for Phillips 66. For context, a PD% above 1.7% would place a company in the top decile of default risk across the S&P 500, based on data as of April 11.

Exhibit 6: StarMine Smart Ratios Model – Phillips 66

Source: LSEG Workspace

As Q1 results approach, the combination of tariff risks, oil supply concerns, and analyst sentiment suggests a more cautious setup for the sector.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chevron: Why The Stars Are Aligning
data.description:
Oil prices felt the brunt of macro selling pressure in the sessions that followed President Trump's White House Rose Garden tariff announcement on Liberation Day, April 2, 2025. WTI plunged to $55 for a time, dropping below key support in the low-mid $60s per barrel. In my technical eye, and as illustrated below, that yields a downside measured move price target to below $30, based on the height of the previous consolidation pattern.

Fundamentally, the Energy sector now trades at just 13x earnings, a historically low P/E ratio. One of the US Energy sector's stalwarts is Chevron (NYSE:CVX) (NEOE:CHEV:CA). I was bullish on the integrated oil & gas company in 2024, and I reiterate a buy rating.

Shares are down 12.7% in total return since my previous assessment, but that is basically in line with the S&P 500 performance. With a favorable valuation and a chart that's close to critical support, now could be the right time to be long shares.

WTI Crude Oil's Chart is Concerning

Energy Sector P/E Hits 13x

Back in February, Chevron reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 2024 non-GAAP EPS of $2.06 missed the Wall Street consensus estimate by a nickel, while revenue of $52.2 billion, up 11% from the same period a year earlier, was a significant $3.8 billion beat. It tallied US and worldwide production increases of 7% and 19%, respectively.

Shares fell by 4.6% in the session that followed, however, and have since trended lower. Ahead of its Q2 profit report, the options market prices in a 4.9% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the May 2 report.

Looking back on the quarter, it was actually soft US chemicals sales and timing effects of ex-US downstream activities that led to the bottom-line miss. Upstream costs that were above forecasts also pressured EPS, though healthy upstream production and international liquids profits helped drive the revenue beat. Cash flow from operations, ex-changes in working capital, was somewhat weak.

Despite some headwinds, the management team kept to its large buyback schedule, and its high dividend yield makes for an attractive total shareholder yield. Now ranked No. 1 out of 17 in its industry by Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system, Chevron signaled optimism to the street in February when it announced a dividend increase.

Operationally, Gulf of America and shale operations should continue to deliver robust free cash flow, but all eyes are on domestic oil prices. Year-end proven reserve totaled 9.8 billion of net oil-equivalent, but its refining business reported its first quarterly loss since its 2020 beginning.

On the call, the management team had a positive outlook despite the sagging oil market-it raised free cash flow guidance along with the dividend hike. Its net debt ratio remains under 10%, while renewables remains part of its long-term strategy.

On the earnings outlook, there has been a steep drop in EPS estimates. For FY 2025, the sellside now sees just $9.97 of per-share earnings-that's down from $11.27 at the time of my previous analysis last December. Still, strong EPS gains are forecast in the out year and through FY 2027.

Much hinges on WTI and Brent-a recovery above $70 per barrel on domestic oil would be a boon, but that may depend on the US side-stepping a recession. As it stands, there have been a high of 14 analyst downgrades of earnings, compared with just six upgrades in the last 90 days. Still, profitability trends are very strong-free cash flow per share is now $8.31 over the past 12 months, which is a 6.1% free cash flow yield.

Chevron: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have some adjustments to my fair value estimate. If we assume normalized forward 12-month non-GAAP EPS of $10.50, below that of my previous assessment, and apply a 15 multiple, then shares should trade near $158.

A mid-teens P/E makes (and might even be too conservative) sense due to trough earnings perhaps happening now and the forecast for big growth in the years ahead. A 15x multiple on FY 2027 EPS of $13.56, for perspective, would yield a stock price of $203 as of the end of 2026.

Chevron: Shares Undervalued Given EPS Growth Ahead, WTI a Wildcard

Key risks include rising costs, supply chain issues amid trade war challenges, and growing competition from smaller and non-US players. Geopolitical risks are ongoing, which potentially puts Chevron's capex plans in jeopardy. Of course, a global recession would likely send WTI and Brent down, which would be quite bearish for CVX shares.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Friday, May 2 BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares undervalued in my view, CVX's chart has its problems but also opportunities. I noted a modest downtrend last year and an overall weak technical setup. That generally holds today, but let's refresh key price points. Notice in the graph below that the stock has dropped to its lowest mark since September last year-a period that features a macro growth scare. The same downtrend support line that I drew last time is in play, now near $128. So, a bit more downside is possible.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum gauge at the top of the chart-it's coming off technical oversold conditions. Moreover, there's a high amount of volume by price from today's level all the way up to $180, putting the onus on the bulls to lift CVX through a major congestion zone. On the upside, the $165 to $170 range is resistance. Overall, the stock is close to major support.

CVX: Shares Near Crucial Support

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on Chevron. I see the Energy large cap as undervalued with attractive shareholder returns (dividends and buybacks). Though the technicals remain challenged, CVX is now approaching long-term support. The stars are aligning between the valuation and technicals, but risks must be monitored.

data.title: EQT Corporation: Look To Safety As U.S. Energy Policy Realigns
data.description:
Background

When looking at US-based natural gas companies it's hard to overlook EQT Corporation (NYSE:EQT). They have the largest production network in the US and have pursued an aggressive stock buyback plan, as well as strategic acquisitions of Tug Hill and XcL Midstream.

Here's where it gets interesting, The Trump administration's tariff policy has excluded natural gas completely. Therefore, shareholders have a unique opportunity to be insulated from economic headwinds balanced by a lean cash flowing gas giant.

In this article, I’ll break down why I think EQT’s in a sweet spot right now. They’re well positioned to win from these tariffs because demand is going to shift further toward domestic producers.

You might be asking, if there aren’t any tariffs on natural gas, why does this even matter for EQT? It’s the ripple effect. Tariffs make everything else more expensive to bring in, and that puts the spotlight back on American producers. Manufacturing is coming home, global supply chains are still a mess, and buyers will start to favor domestic energy just to keep things simple and reliable. On top of that, LNG demand overseas keeps climbing, which tightens supply here and keeps prices from falling too far. EQT’s got the size, the network, and the right timing.

Competitive Advantages from Operations

Currently EQT operates out of the Marcellus and Utica shale formations, which span portions of Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Ohio. This broad geographic footprint across the Appalachian Basin helps give a good picture of the large influence they have in the region.

The Appalachian Basin is home to a variety of natural gas operators but none rival EQT. Antero Resources (AR) is the closest in terms of cost structure with a production breakeven of $2.19/MMBtu. Keep in mind recent spot prices have been around $4.05/MMBtu which means EQT has 10.5% more profit per MMBtu than Antero Resources.

Additionally EQT’s measure of 8 Bcfe/d (billion cubic feet equivalent of natural gas per day), which far exceeds both Antero Resources at 3.4 Bcfe/d, and Range Resources (RRC) at 2.2 Bcfe/d as of Q4 2024.

By operating at such a massive scale and dominating the region, they are able to have large economies of scale, lowering their costs compared to competitors.

EQT’s liquidity profile is a key reason the company is well equipped to survive turbulent market conditions. With a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.7x EQT maintains a conservative approach to leverage. Paired with an undrawn credit facility of 1.5 billion, the company has financial flexibility and a solid capital foundation. The financial not only insulates EQT from volatility, but also positions it to capitalize on shifting domestic policy.

US-Centric Resource Policy

When evaluating the new administration's energy policy it's crucial to look at the new executive order targeting state overreach on emissions standards and other potential bottlenecks for US producers.

According to a direct statement from the White House: “The Attorney General will prioritize taking action against laws and policies purporting to address ‘climate change’ policies, or involving ‘environmental, social, and governance’ initiatives, ‘environmental justice,’ carbon or ‘greenhouse gas’ emissions, and funds to collect carbon penalties or carbon taxes.”

Source: (White House Protects American Energy from State Overreach)

This quote highlights several key points that are especially important for investors to consider.

  1. The new administration is focused on making the production of oil and gas in the US more friendly.
  2. Additionally they want US suppliers to buy from US producers and create more ‘energy security’ as they have called it.
  3. With the broad breakdown of the ESG consensus amongst the financial and political world investing in cash flowing commodity assets will be crucial if we enter into an economic downturn.

Positioning for the New World Order

Let’s dive deeper into the Q4 2024 report because I believe there are some real gems that the market hasn’t fully recognized.

Beyond EQT’s day-to-day operational edge over its peers, it’s important to examine how capital expenditures have been allocated to get a clearer picture of the company’s positioning.

The focus on upstream maintenance and lack of ‘cowboy exploration’ has given EQT the time to generate economies of scale that other producers can’t match.

Note the financial sensitives for 2025 paint a very clear growth picture. With FY 2024 EBITDA coming at 3.37 billion, the company is projecting a 57.3% increase to 5.3 billion if they hit the low end of the $4.00 MMBtu spot price mark. This is much larger than the 23.5% YoY EBITDA gain for EQT from FY 23 to FY 24.

Even though EQT has efficient operations, a strong forecast, and political tailwinds there are very clear risks with any investment that investors need to keep in mind.

Risks and Threats to Thesis

At its core, natural gas is a volatile industry with dynamic pricing that can beat down even the shrewdest of operators. Even though EQT holds a significant cost advantage when it comes to drilling, it doesn’t always mean Mr. Market will cooperate.

In November 2024 the natural gas spot price fell below $1.5MMBtu. This is well below EQT’s breakeven but even more so for their closest competitors. The sudden drop in price was related to warm November weather throughout most of the north and southeast. It also didn’t help that natural gas storage was high which pushed prices. Simply put, supply outclassed demand.

Risks and Threats to the Thesis

EBITDA margin percentage is one of the most reliable ways to compare companies in the natural gas space. When evaluating peer companies, it’s important to get the most 1:1 comparisons available.

Company Adj. EBITDA (Millions) Revenue (Millions) EBITDA Margin (%)
EQT $1,142 $1821 77.6%
Antero Resources $322 $1,120 29.6%
Range Resources $350 $626 55.9%

Source: (Antero Resources 2024 10-K SEC Filing)

Source: (Range Resources 2024 10-K SEC Filing)

Appalachian Basin producers are all in steady positions given recent strength in prices. As shown in the chart above, EQT stands out amongst peers for its ability to convert superior free cash flow into earnings. However, there is always room for macroeconomic headwinds, and maintaining consistent earnings is best hedge.

Earnings Preview

EQT is set to report Q1 earnings on April 22nd after market close. Analysts are looking for $1.01 per share, up from the 82 cents EQT reported the same quarter last year. With four consecutive earnings beats, another strong report would hammer home to investors their ability to generate free cash flow and deliver consistent returns.

Source: (Nasdaq: EQT Q1 2025 Earnings Preview)

Summary

In commodity sectors, macroeconomic influence plays a major role in driving stock performance. Keeping that in mind, it’s important to look at inconsistencies within market segments that determine how well a company can adjust compared to its peers in the industry. I rate EQT a buy given the strong base of production it has built and the loosening of government regulations surrounding natural gas.

data.title: Federal Budget: Q1 2025 - DOGE Has Not Moved The Needle
data.description:
Federal Budget

The Federal Government publishes the spending and revenue numbers on a monthly basis. The charts and tables below give an in depth review of the Federal Budget, showing where the money is coming from, where it is going to, and the surplus or deficit.

The government fiscal year closes at the end of September, but this analysis will look at the calendar year budget. As shown below, the last three months were all deficits exceeding -$100B with February showing worse than -$300B.

Figure: 1 Monthly Federal Budget

On average, January and February were much worse than history.

Figure: 2 Current vs Historical

We can look at the quarter in aggregate using the Sankey diagram below that shows the $596B deficit, which means the US has a quarterly shortfall of revenue equal to 33.6% of expenses!

DOGE has been unable to move the needle despite all the blustering. Looking at the diagram below, DOGE is not even going after any of the big items: Social Security, Health, Medicare, Net Interest, Income Security, and National Defense. Without going after these massive spending programs, DOGE will only make a lot of noise without accomplishing anything.

Figure: 3 Quarterly Federal Budget Sankey

This latest quarter was not as bad as Q4 2024, but was still a very bad quarter.

Figure: 4 Quarterly Historical Deficit/Surplus for Previous Five Years

The next two charts show the quarterly revenue and costs broken down by expense type. You can see how the most recent quarter generated a little bit more revenue than last quarter. This was mainly driven by an increase in Individual Income Taxes.

Figure: 5 Quarterly Receipts

On the flip side, you can see that spending was slightly below last quarter, but not in a meaningful way.

Figure: 6 Quarterly Outlays

Interest Expense has ballooned higher to $941B.

Figure: 7 TTM Interest Expense

Historical Perspective

Zooming out and looking over the history of the budget back to 1980 shows a complete picture. The change since Covid is quite dramatic.

Figure: 8 Trailing 12 Months (TTM)

The next two charts zoom in on the recent periods to show the change when compared to pre-Covid. These charts show spending and revenue on a trailing 12-month basis period over period. While the last 12 months was a record revenue number, but spending is still growing faster.

Figure: 9 Annual Federal Receipts

Figure: 10 Annual Federal Expenses

Finally, we finish with a Sankey showing TTM Deficit was $2.07B which is a massive issue going forward, representing 29% of all government spending. The government cannot afford to keep printing such massive debt figures.

Figure: 11 TTM Federal Budget Sankey

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FTCS: Unconvincing Quality-Focused ETF
data.description:
FTCS strategy

First Trust Capital Strength ETF (NASDAQ:FTCS) started investing operations on 7/6/2006 and tracks the Capital Strength Index. FTCS has a portfolio of 50 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.94% and an expense ratio of 0.54%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described by First Trust, the methodology starts from the Nasdaq US Benchmark Index and keeps the 500 securities with the largest float-adjusted market capitalization and a minimum 3-month average daily volume of $5 million. Additionally, eligible stocks must have at least $1 billion in cash or short-term investments, a long-term debt to market cap ratio below 30% and a return on equity above 15%. They are ranked by a volatility score combining trailing intervals of 3 months and 12 months. The 50 companies with the lowest volatility score are selected in the index, which is reconstituted quarterly with constituents in equal weight and a maximum of 30% in any industry.

Portfolio

FTCS is almost exclusively invested in US companies (98% of asset value), mostly in large and mega caps (65%). Therefore, this article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

The portfolio has a focus on industrials (27.5%) and significant exposure in financials (18.9%) and consumer staples (15.7%). Compared to the S&P 500, FTCS mostly overweights industrials and consumer staples, while it massively underweights technology, communication and ignores energy, utilities, and real estate.

The portfolio is diversified and risks related to individual companies are low. Positions are reset in equal weight at each rebalancing. The current top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 22.9% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 2.4%. These are the constituents with the highest price return since the most recent rebalancing.

Takeaway

First Trust Capital Strength ETF has a portfolio of 50 stocks meeting quality and low volatility criteria. FTCS is well-diversified due to its equal-weight methodology and has a focus on industrials. FTCS has lagged the S&P 500 since its inception, mostly due to underperformance in the last few years. Several quality ETFs with lower fees have significantly outperformed FTCS since 2018.

data.title: Rollins: Great Business With An Unreasonable Valuation
data.description:
2025 has been a rough year for most stocks, especially after President Trump imposed his tariffs and later paused them. Still, even with his pause, the market doesn't seem reassured as both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq continue their downward spiral. YTD the S&P 500 is down over 8% while the Nasdaq is down more than 11%.

While many stocks may be on sale, some certainly are not and Rollins, Inc. (NYSE:ROL) is one that I can assure you is not. As many of my followers likely know, one of my favorite investors is Terry Smith, CEO of Fundsmith, and arguably one of the best investors over the last few decades. Smith has a very simple investing philosophy, find high-quality businesses, don't overpay then do nothing.

While Rollins is a very high-quality business, I believe it's currently not reasonably priced. Let's dig into the business and discuss why the company isn't trading at an attractive valuation.

The Company, Moat, and Future Opportunities

Rollins, Inc. has a rich history as the company began in 1948. Two brothers, Wayne and John Rollins, started Rollins Broadcasting, which was as it sounds more a radio and broadcasting business. However, the brothers made a pivot when they acquired Orkin Exterminating Company in 1965. The brothers then changed the name of their business to Rollins, Inc. and began trading as a public company on the New York Stock Exchange in 1968. Since the initial IPO, Rollins has become a powerhouse in the pest control business as there are over 800 company owned and franchised locations in roughly 70 countries.

While Orkin is the company's most noticeable brand, Rollins has an umbrella of meaningful brands that are driving the business forward, as you can see below:

However, Orkin remains the strongest brand in the company's portfolio and as their slogan suggests, they are "The Best in Pests" as Orkin is #1 in brand awareness when it comes to pest control in the United States. For that reason, 50% of customers come directly to Orkin. This graphic below illustrates the power of the Orkin brand as it had far more monthly website traffic compared to competitors in 2023:

For these reasons, I do believe Rollins has a moat in the pest control industry as it's very much a top of mind company when it comes to pest control.

Rollins believes they have an opportunity to at least double their current market served as shown below:

I would agree with many of Rollins' stated tailwinds and don't think it's unreasonable the company can grow by 9% to 11% next year. Most of that growth will come from organic growth in the hopes residential and commercial revenues can continue to grow.

As this table from the company's recent 10K filing illustrates, Rollins has steadily been growing not only in residential and commercial revenue but in termite completions, bait monitoring, renewals and other ancillary services as well:

I think this can continue as compared to Baby Boomers, I think Millennials and younger generations are less likely to do-it-themselves. For that reason, Rollins has an opportunity to increase prices given this is an essential service which could also lead to margin expansion.

From a commercial perspective, it makes sense that companies would need to comply with regulatory environmental standards and in general wish to curb the amount of pests surrounding their facilities, especially mosquitos and other pests known to carry viruses.

From a growth perspective, one reason I believe Rollins can continue to grow is because of acquisitions. In 2024, the company had 44 tuck-in deals and on the company's last earnings call, management stated, "We have a robust M&A pipeline with a number of opportunities that we are actively evaluating to drive additional growth."

As a successful serial acquirer of pest related firms, Rollins has a history of making successful deals which led to revenue growth and there are plenty of additional opportunities as noted by this graphic from a prior investor relations presentation:

For those reasons, I think Rollins' goal of 2%-3% revenue growth from M&A seems achievable.

Lastly, I'd like to note I think there is an opportunity for margin expansion as artificial intelligence will likely create operational efficiencies. As shown in the graphic below, 60% of Rollins' current SG&A expenses are admin related; thus, there is certainly potential for AI to streamline internal processes:

On the last conference call, management emphasized training and a focus of continued safety education, the company's CEO Jerry Gahlhoff stated, "We made positive strides in this area throughout 2024 by updating our training and onboarding programs to better address key milestones in the early days that a new team member is with us. Another important part of our culture is our dedication to continuous improvement and our focus on safety is a key element of this. There's nothing more important than ensuring our teammates to make it home safely at the end of the day. And in 2024, we implemented several new programs to continue building our safety culture here at Rollins."

These trainings will hopefully create capable, knowledgeable employees and also reduce the company's insurance claims.

Management

Jerry E. Gahlhoff, Jr. is the CEO of Rollins. Gahlhoff took over the role of CEO in 2023 from long-time CEO Gary Rollins (the son of Wayne Rollins). Despite only being at the helm for a short time, Gahlhoff has worked at Rollins for over 25 years and has held a variety of leadership positions.

The company's CFO is Kenneth Krause. He has held this position since 2022. Prior to working at Rollins, Krause was the CFO at MSA Safety, Inc. and he also worked at KPMG.

I believe company culture can play a role in the long-term results of a company. The Glassdoor reviews for Rollins are okay, with most employees stating they would recommend the company to a friend. The company's employees do seem to approve of Gahlhoff as he has a nearly 80% approval rating as you can see below:

I like when the management of a company has skin in the game. Gahlhoff doesn't own a material amount of shares, but the Rollins family certainly does. In the company's latest proxy filing, Gary Rollins owns roughly 3% of all outstanding shares and his daughter Pamela Rollins owns 1.22% of all outstanding shares. Plus, the Rollins family has shares in Significant Shareholder Group (which is composed of Rollins family members), the Gary W. Rollins Voting Trust, and the R. Randall Rollins Voting Trust. In total, the Rollins family owns more than 50% of all outstanding shares and thus certainly have a great deal of interest in the long-term success of the business.

Financials

As one might expect, Rollins has a very dependable and successful business model. As this graphic from a prior investor relations presentation illustrates, Rollins has been able to continuous grow revenue even through some of the most difficult economic times, such as the financial crisis of 2008 and during the COVID-19 pandemic:

In fiscal year 2024, Rollins generated roughly revenue of $3.4 billion, which is an increase of over 10% compared to 2023. 7.9% of this growth was organic, while roughly 3% of growth was related to acquisitions. For 2024, operating income increased by 12.7% compared to 2023, net income increased by over 7%, and GAAP earnings per share increased by nearly 8%.

As you can see below, these are certainly impressive figures as revenue, operating income, net income, and earnings per share have all increased since 2022:

Furthermore, I think shareholders would appreciate that weighted average shares outstanding have declined since 2022 and the company's amount of dividends paid per share has increased. In 2024, Rollins paid dividends to shareholders totaling $298 million.

The company is able to continue to increase their dividend payments and still make appropriate acquisitions as the business generated operating cash flows of $608 million which is an increase of 15% compared to 2023 and free cash flow for the year grew to $580 million as you can see below:

I think this graphic clearly illustrates Rollins is doing an excellent job of generating cash, and shareholders are being rewarded through dividends as well as buybacks.

Rollins has a decent Balance Sheet as you can see below, the company doesn't have enough current assets to cover all the company's liabilities:

I also don't love that from an asset perspective, the company is very goodwill heavy, which could lead to impairment charges. However, as Rollins is a solid cash generating company, I'm not too worried about the company's current liabilities or long-term debt as the business has a good debt to equity balance with retained earnings continuing to grow.

Risks

Rollins has numerous risks, many of which are mentioned in the risk factors section of the company's 10K filing. I'm going to discuss three which to me could impact the business over the long term.

The first risk is competition. As I noted above, I do believe Rollins has a moat in the pest control market, especially as Orkin is a very well-known and respected brand. That being said, the pest control business is one with low barriers to entry and this is a fragmented industry given the different pests. Aside from other national brands such as Aptive Environment, Rollin also faces many other regional and local competitors. However, Rollins is in an excellent position to acquire a competitor should they wish to do so with their capital.

This leads me to my next risk, which is failure to integrate or make appropriate acquisitions. In general, I'm not too concerned about this risk as Rollins has a history of making appropriate acquisitions, and they have a disciplined and strategic approach to their M&A activity. Rollins has been a successful acquirer for years and I imagine they will continue to make appropriate well-suited acquisitions in the years to come. However, an unsuccessful integration of an acquisition could lead to additional costs and hurt the business's bottom line.

The final risk I'd like to mention is climate change. There is a risk that as the climate changes in some regions of the United States, the services Rollins provides might not be as necessary. Now, while in some areas that may be true, this shift in weather dynamics might generate new opportunities for the business as an increase in temperature could bring out more rodents and insects in some regions of the country.

I also think Rollins's CEO made a good point on the company's latest earnings call when asked about changes in weather and some severe weather conditions, "It's interesting you think about insects and related pests. They're all very adaptive. A lot of mechanisms to survive difficult conditions. You think about - one example, I'll give you, is I think about mosquitoes in Minnesota. Mosquitoes, in Minnesota, can be really bad once you get to spring or summer. And guess what, they'll probably be really bad again this year because those mosquitoes are able to over winter in immature stages, they're able to over winter and do just fine because they're back. Back in the late spring, they're back biting people."

Overall, I'm not too concerned about climate change for Rollins because, as Gahlhoff noted, bugs are adaptive and even if one species is put in check another may quickly replace it, as is the natural way of the world.

Valuation

Rollins has a valuation grade of a "F" at Seeking Alpha:

As you can clearly see, whatever your preferred valuation metric, Rollins is an expensive stock. For me, as Rollins is a profitable and established business, I believe price to earnings is the best valuation metric. On both a trailing and forward P/E GAAP basis, currently Rollins far exceeds the sector median. It's not entirely shocking that Rollins has much higher P/E compared to the sector median, as it's a high-quality business and thus many investors are willing to pay up. However, even on a historic basis, the current forward and trailing P/E GAAP ratios for Rollins are higher than the company's five-year averages.

From another viewpoint, in using a reverse discounted cash flow model with a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3% Rollins would need assumed growth of over 18% over the next ten years to justify the current share price:

As most analysts believe, Rollins can grow in the high single digits to lower double digits over the next few years, which, I think, is far more realistic compared to 18.5%. I think Rollins is currently an overvalued stock, and wouldn't consider buying shares until the stock price reached December or January levels.

Conclusion

There is a lot to like with Rollins, Inc. and I can see why Terry Smith is a fan and holder of this company. It's a profitable, well-run business that is generating more cash flow as revenues continue to grow through organic growth and M&A activity.

However, even though this is a high-quality business, it's one that's priced at a premium and one that hasn't fallen in 2025 despite the market turmoil.

I have a small position in Rollins as I believe it's a company with a moat in the pest control industry, but given Rollins' current valuation I'm not looking to add shares.

data.title: Resilience And Risk: Navigating CRE Valuations In An Unprecedented Tariff-Laden Environment
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

Private commercial real estate (CRE) markets face a fresh wave of uncertainty following the announcement of sweeping new tariffs on U.S. imports that have been on-again and off-again in recent days.

We are sympathetic to questions regarding what’s next for private CRE prices as the modern CRE market, which, as we know it today, didn’t exist 100 years ago when trade levies were last at these levels.

There isn’t a historical playbook to anchor views. In such times, especially given very fluid market dynamics today, it’s helpful to outline a framework that evaluates the potential impact on the CRE market.

  • Our base case calls for unlevered CRE total returns to muddle along as income returns help offset a decline in capital returns.
  • Our bull case calls for high single-digit total returns as tariff tensions de-escalate. The best returns historically occur in the aftermath of significant drawdowns like what has already occurred.
  • Our bear case sees total returns falling an additional -10% as the market prices capital return declines like the S&L crisis/GFC as interest rates are shocked higher.

Keeping fundamentals and a framework in focus amid the time of tariffs

Valuations are already on the defensive: Private unlevered CRE capital returns (i.e., prices) have already declined roughly 20% from their peak in late 2Q 2022 (as measured by the NCREIF Property Index or NPI), driven by the sharp reset in interest rates (and have only recently turned positive over the past couple of quarters).

While that, in and of itself, is not a reason to say that property prices will be insulated from the impact of tariffs, we note that it’s an atypical reaction for CRE prices to reset lower while the economy was on a firmer footing.

In other words, we think the CRE market has already priced in a shock that many other markets have not. As we look forward, we believe there are three important factors to consider about this downturn.

First, the new supply of buildings is set to decline rapidly: It’s important to remember that a key historical catalyst for declining CRE valuations is an overbuilding of commercial real estate leading into an economic downturn.

New development of CRE is driven by rising demand for space, pushing net operating income (NOI) growth higher while lending conditions are loose. These conditions do not exist across most property types today, as CRE has been an asset class that has been out of favor for more than two years. Indeed, the supply of new construction was expected to decline significantly in the coming years, even before the recently announced tariffs.

Second, NOI growth is expected to slow but remain positive: While a weakening economy may lead NOI growth to decelerate from today’s mid-3% range to below the historical average of 2.5% to 3%, we don’t expect it to turn decidedly negative as it has in other economic downturns.

Commercial real estate is largely insulated from the direct impacts of tariffs, and the contractual long-term leases create more durable cash flows. Furthermore, CRE is a less cyclical asset class than 15-20 years ago, given the emergence of new property types.

For instance, 60% of the U.S. REIT market cap is now concentrated in NextGen property types such as data centers, cell towers, single-family rentals, and seniors housing. In our view, it would take a significant bankruptcy shock to change this fundamental backdrop.

Third, CRE balance sheets are generally healthy: The balance sheets of commercial real estate owners are on much firmer footing than they were during the Global Financial Crisis.

For instance, U.S.-listed REITs have loan-to-value ratios of approximately 35%, and open-ended funds that own core commercial real estate (i.e., ODCE funds) are even lower, at less than 30%. We see signs of stable liquidity in the CRE debt markets as lenders are still comfortable lending on valuations that have reset lower, although they are backing up spreads given market volatility.

Our base, bearish, and bullish cases for CRE in unusual times

Against this backdrop, our base case is that property valuations muddle through 2025. While capital returns may face headwinds in the coming quarters, income returns of ~1% per quarter are an overlooked offset.

This may seem bullish given recent developments, but it was not too long ago that some hoped for a more robust recovery, as the best returns for U.S. commercial real estate historically occur in the aftermath of significant drawdowns such as what we’ve seen since late 2022.

Real estate may be a relative haven in these volatile markets: it’s the sixth best of the 11 S&P sectors year-to-date as of the week ending April 11, 2025, and REITs are outperforming the S&P 500 by approximately +358 bp and +834 bp versus the NASDAQ so far in 2025.

That said, we anticipate inconsistent performance across property types, with industrial, hotels, and discretionary retail likely facing the most significant headwinds. At the same time, seniors housing, single-family rental, and manufactured housing are better positioned in this tariff climate.

Our bearish case is a rising interest rate shock where lending conditions tighten and NOI growth meaningfully slows. This is most akin to stagflation, which has historically not been a constructive backdrop for CRE.

We think this has the potential to pressure CRE unlevered total returns an additional -10% lower, bringing cumulative capital return declines since 2022’s peak to more than -30%.

As a reference, during the S&L crisis of the early 1990s, unlevered capital returns (i.e., prices) declined by -32% with total returns of -11%, and during the GFC, unlevered capital returns fell by -32% with total returns down -24%. At the risk of whistling past the graveyard, today’s environment does not feel as severe as these two prior environments as we aren’t dealing with a CRE credit crisis.

And for investors who may be wondering how CRE performed in the late 1990s when the Russian Debt Crisis shocked 10-year treasury rates higher, private CRE valuations continued to rise as the sector was still in the relatively early innings of recovering from the S&L crisis (not unlike today).

On the other hand, our bullish case scenario for CRE sees unlevered returns rising in line with the historical average based on rolling average 10-year CAGRs of ~8%. This is predicated on NOI growth remaining relatively stable, improving lending conditions, and interest rates declining as tariff tension de-escalates.

CRE outperforms in this scenario because it is not only a haven in such a scenario, but also cheap given the current drawdown from peak. While the damage may have already been done to the economy, there’s precedent for U.S. CRE to outperform even as the U.S. moves into a recession and risk assets sell off, as evidenced by the early 2000s.

Capital markets may be volatile over the short term, but we underscore that abstracting their reactions is not always a good idea for projecting longer-term valuations and fundamentals. That seems especially true during unprecedented times such as these.
